author: @maxkennedy5073
text: 1st off the science stuff, especially the doing, is great.  Watched quite a few of the battery and supercapacitor video's and am wondering if you've covered building a simple charger or chargers for the different voltages and amps?  Your desktop apparatus is wonderful but a bit dear for most of us.  I'm going oldest to newest so if that's been covered later, wonderbar.  If not is there such a thing or things or something inexpensive that serves the purpose?  Something that would prevent overcharging and damaging what is being worked on.  2nd, though it seems to be implied I'd like to ask permission to use your video's in classrooms.  I teach science across the pond in Canada and very much agree with the idea of learning by doing.  I happen to think your video's would be quite inspirational.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: mate - i don't mean to get at you - but people have no idea what is involved here - they ask that me and steve produce batteries at commercial scale - there are only two of us in a small unit in Canterbury working all hours to get ths right and they ask us to perform as if there were two hundred of us with unlimited funds - have you seen folks posting - 'oh your just selling' - how do they think we raise funds? - what do they think we do with them? - it's all stacked against us - we have to produce commercial grade product with out of the scope performance at commercial quantaties on a budget as tight as can be - we do our best and we are getting there but you just can't expect the same timelines - actually if you think about it we are incredibly fast - normally it would take a team of 40 plus 3-5 years to do what we have done.

can I mix cersulfat and potassiumhexacyanoferrat II  together without getting very toxic ?


thanks.


gerhard

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate - I don't know - cerumium sulphate is noted as a redox additive in the research papers and hexacyanoferrates are both redox additives and used to form host matrixes for multivalent ion insertion and deinsertion but i haven't read a paper combining the two nor tried it myself - so i just don't know i am afraid

author: @styleslider400
text:
cool video some good info thanks for sharing Robert, hey i have a question for you...
is it possible to polarize an insulating material 
eg. have plastic type material sitting in a strong electric field created from 2 steel plates ( big air capacitor ) with positive wire connected on one plate and negative charge wire on the other plate,
while the plastic is in a hot melted liquid form and the strong electric field is active would the plastic polarize in relation to the field and if we start to let the plastic cool down and return back to its frozen /solid form would the plastic material hold a polarized effect?
im thinking if it is even possible, that you could somehow polarize a sheet of plastic. would this increase the dielectric constant of the material as long as you have the sheet facing the correct way in relation to the capacitor plates polarity.
and would you still retain the dielectric strength properties of that material?
the thought behind this is how magnets work as they can hold there magnetic dipole structure if they stay cool (frozen / welded state), but if they get really hot ( closer to melting / liquid form temperature ) they seem to de-magnetize possibly being that the atom structure becomes free to move back to an original equilibrium state,.
what are you thoughts on this? 
do you think that something like this is even possible to do?
i thought i would ask as i cant seem to find a great deal of info about this topic.
thanks steve

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: electrets are made in a very similar way mate - i might make one on a video

replies.author: @styleslider400
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering thanks for the reply, that would be awesome to see as i have never heard of an electret before, if you do end up making a video on this topic im kinda interested to see if this method can be used with some insulators that are physically strong and don't dissolve in water such as plastic.cheers mate

author: @rocketred55555
text: Hi Rob, People might also try Calcium Chloride. SAFE to use and much higher ionic strength than table salt and much less temperature sensitive. Can obtain a very high concentration which does not freeze - the reason used on roads - not able to freeze so crystals will not wreck your capacitor. Calcium chloride is cheap as chips from local hardware - used for drying air in cupboards. Make an almost saturated solution at room temperature i.e. add as much as possible to saturate until undissolved remains after stirring. Drain off the saturated and add about 10% water (then won't freeze. If saturated at room temp, then cooling pushes it into crystals at lower temp. Little bit extra water stops this.).  You can place final capacitor in freezer and should come out and still  work great. Then try leaving in sun to get to say 40C and compare. Good luck. (Would be great if people could post their results comparing NaCl with CaCl.) Do not put the NaCl one in freezer, likely to freeze. Suggest put the solution in freezer first ..if it freezes, warm up add a little more water until it doesn't.

author: @boots7859
text:
Interesting. So, if one lived by the ocean and set up several of those along the in/out tide line, one could make a somewhat constant psu?
Or, could one make one simply with an entrance and exit, submerge below water line and allow normal water movement to constantly refresh the saltwater electrolyte?
Would require regular maint. for algae of course, however I could see a package like this as an emergency power source for near ocean hiking maybe.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text: I think that there might be an issue with the electrolyte from one cell being in contact with the electrolyte in another cell. In lead acid batteries, the cells are separated so that the fluid does not circulate between them. The only contact is between electrodes.

replies.text: I should iterate on my last comment. If a new super cap has the same energy storage as your today's type battery, then no it will not last longer between charges.
